name: Union Executive and Union Legislature

sourceRefs.label: 2025_q1 page 53 Q112 (NOT-correct statement about the President — continuance-in-office trap, with answer key)
2025_q1 page 100 Q165 (Table/Warrant of Precedence ordering, with answer key)
2026_q page 31 Q30 (Assertion-Reason on PM's role in appointing Ministers, with answer key)

vajiramandravi.com/lawbhoomi.com - electoral college composition, Article 61 impeachment procedure

researchNotes: 4 real hits plus 1 previously-stashed real hit (the VP qualification trap from Day 28's research) — all reused here since they belong together as the Union Executive cluster. Real exam evidence shows a strong trap pattern around 'what people assume vs. what's actually true': the President's term/continuance rule, the Vice-President's eligibility house, and the PM's actual role in appointing Ministers are all tested via deliberately wrong-sounding-plausible statements rather than plain recall.

reading.intro: Covers the President, Vice-President, Prime Minister and Council of Ministers (Union Executive), and briefly the Union Legislature/Parliament's composition. Real exam evidence shows a recurring trap pattern: statements that sound correct but get one specific detail wrong (term continuance, which House, who's actually responsible for what).

qa.question: How is the President of India elected, and who can vote in this election?
qa.answer: By an Electoral College consisting of the ELECTED members of both Houses of Parliament and the ELECTED members of the Legislative ASSEMBLIES of the States and of the Union Territories of Delhi and Puducherry (NOT nominated members of Parliament, NOT members of Legislative COUNCILS, and not most other UTs). Election uses the system of proportional representation by means of the single transferable vote, by secret ballot.

qa.question: Real exam: Which statement about the President is NOT correct — that the President cannot hold office beyond the 5-year term, can resign anytime, can be removed by impeachment, or has never actually been impeached?
qa.answer: 'The President cannot hold office beyond his term of five years' is the NOT-correct (false) statement — under Article 56(1)(c), the President, despite the 5-year term expiring, continues to hold office until a successor enters office. The other three statements are all true: the President can resign at any time (addressed to the Vice-President), can be removed via impeachment (Article 61), and no Indian President has ever actually been impeached.

qa.question: What is the procedure for impeaching the President under Article 61?
qa.answer: A charge of violating the Constitution is preferred by either House of Parliament, with at least 14 days' written notice signed by not less than 1/4th of that House's total membership; the resolution must pass by a 2/3rds majority of that House's total membership. The OTHER House then investigates the charge (President has the right to appear/be represented); if THAT House also passes the charge by a 2/3rds majority of its total membership, the President stands removed from the date of that resolution.

qa.question: Real exam: What are the qualifications to be Vice-President, and what is the common eligibility trap?
qa.answer: Must be a citizen of India and have completed 35 years of age — both TRUE requirements. The trap: a real exam tested 'qualified for election as a member of the LOK SABHA' as a FALSE requirement — the actual constitutional requirement (Article 66(3)) is qualification for election to the RAJYA SABHA, not the Lok Sabha.

qa.question: Real exam: In the Table (Warrant) of Precedence, what is the correct relative order among the Vice-President, Speaker of the Lok Sabha, Chief Ministers of States, and Ministers of State of the Union?
qa.answer: Vice-President, then Speaker of the Lok Sabha, then Chief Ministers of States (within their own state), then Ministers of State of the Union — confirmed by a real exam question (this order is consistent with the President ranking 1st overall, the Vice-President 2nd, the Prime Minister 3rd, and so on down the full Warrant of Precedence).

qa.question: Real exam (Assertion-Reason): Is the Prime Minister responsible for appointing Ministers and allocating work among them?
qa.answer: YES, the Prime Minister IS responsible for this (recommends Ministers for the President's formal appointment, and allocates portfolios/work among them) — a real exam's Assertion claiming the PM is 'NOT responsible' for this was tested as FALSE, while the accompanying Reason (PM is leader of the majority party in the Lok Sabha and Head of the Council of Ministers) was tested as TRUE but not a valid explanation of the (false) Assertion.

qa.question: Which Articles cover the Council of Ministers' role, and what is the core constitutional principle behind it?
qa.answer: Article 74: there shall be a Council of Ministers headed by the Prime Minister to AID AND ADVISE the President, who acts in accordance with such advice (making the President largely a constitutional/nominal head, with REAL executive power resting with the Council of Ministers). Article 75: covers appointment of the PM and other Ministers, their term, salaries, and the Council's COLLECTIVE RESPONSIBILITY to the Lok Sabha.

qa.question: What are the two Houses of Parliament, and what is a defining structural feature of each?
qa.answer: Lok Sabha (House of the People) — directly elected by the people, maximum 552 members, 5-year term unless dissolved earlier. Rajya Sabha (Council of States) — represents the States, maximum 250 members, a PERMANENT body that never fully dissolves (one-third of members retire every 2 years, each serving a 6-year term).

qa.question: What is a 'Money Bill', and which House has the final say over it?
qa.answer: A bill dealing exclusively with taxation, government borrowing, or expenditure from the Consolidated Fund (Article 110). It can ONLY be introduced in the Lok Sabha, and the Rajya Sabha has no power to reject or amend it — it may only recommend changes within 14 days, which the Lok Sabha is free to accept or reject entirely.

questions.prompt: The President of India is elected by an Electoral College consisting of the elected members of both Houses of Parliament and the elected members of:
options: State Legislative Assemblies and the UTs of Delhi and Puducherry
Only the Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha
questions.explanation: Elected members of State Legislative Assemblies and the Legislative Assemblies of Delhi and Puducherry.

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about the President of India is NOT correct?
options: The President can resign from office at any time
The President cannot hold office beyond the term of five years
The President can be removed from office by an impeachment process
No President of India has so far been impeached
questions.explanation: False — the President continues in office beyond the 5-year term until a successor takes charge.

questions.prompt: Under Article 61, a charge of impeachment against the President must first be preferred by a House of Parliament with notice signed by at least what fraction of that House's total membership, and passed by what majority?
questions.explanation: 1/4th of members for notice; passed by 2/3rds majority of total membership.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is a genuine qualification to become Vice-President of India?
options: Qualified for election as a member of the Lok Sabha
Qualified for election as a member of the Rajya Sabha
Must have served as a Governor
Must be at least 25 years of age
questions.explanation: Qualified for election to the Rajya Sabha (not Lok Sabha).

questions.prompt: In the Table of Precedence, which of the following is the CORRECT order?
options: Ministers of State of the Union, Chief Ministers of States, Speaker of the Lok Sabha, Vice-President
Vice-President, Speaker of the Lok Sabha, Chief Ministers of States, Ministers of State of the Union
Vice-President, Ministers of State of the Union, Speaker of the Lok Sabha, Chief Ministers of States
Vice-President, Speaker of the Lok Sabha, Ministers of State of the Union, Chief Ministers of States
questions.explanation: Vice-President, Speaker of the Lok Sabha, Chief Ministers of States, Ministers of State of the Union.

questions.prompt: Assertion [A]: The Prime Minister is NOT responsible for the appointment of Ministers and allocation of work among them. Reason [R]: The Prime Minister is the leader of the majority party in the Lok Sabha and is the Head of the Council of Ministers. Choose the correct option.
options: Both [A] and [R] are true, and [R] explains [A]
Both [A] and [R] are true, but [R] does not explain [A]
questions.explanation: [A] is false (the PM IS responsible for this); [R] is true on its own.

questions.prompt: Which Article establishes the Council of Ministers, headed by the Prime Minister, to aid and advise the President?
The Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to which body?
A Money Bill (Article 110) can only be introduced in which House?
questions.trick: Money Bills are Lok-Sabha-exclusive at introduction — Rajya Sabha can only recommend changes within 14 days, never block or amend directly.

questions.prompt: Rajya Sabha members are elected for a 6-year term, with what fraction of members retiring every 2 years?
questions.explanation: One-third retire every 2 years, making it a permanent (never fully dissolved) body.
